dimensions.name: Concept boundary correctness
dimensions.description: Does each block contain exactly one cohesive concept or topic? Are concept transitions used as block boundaries? Does no single block mix two unrelated major topics?

anchors.1: Blocks cut mid-concept — a single concept is split across two blocks, or two unrelated major concepts are merged into one block. The learner would be confused about what a block is about.
anchors.5: Most blocks contain one concept but at least one block has a noticeable topic mismatch — for example, the Waterfall model and Agile model sharing one block, or an introductory section merged with a detailed technical section.
anchors.10: Every block contains exactly one cohesive concept. Block boundaries fall precisely at topic transitions. A learner can state the single topic of every block in one sentence.

dimensions.name: Merge and split rule adherence
dimensions.description: Were the merge rules (small/related H2s joined) and split rules (long H2s with 4+ H3s broken up) applied correctly? Were H3s kept inside their parent H2 except when splitting was justified?

anchors.1: Merge/split rules completely ignored — either every H2 became its own block regardless of size and relationship, or completely unrelated sections were merged. H3s split away from parent H2 without justification.
anchors.5: Rules partially applied — one obvious merge was missed (e.g. a tiny intro section left as its own block) or one obvious split was missed (e.g. a 700-word section with 6 H3s kept as one block). H3 separation mostly correct.
anchors.10: All merge and split rules applied precisely. Tightly related small H2s are merged. Oversized H2 sections with 4+ H3s are split at a logical H3 boundary. Every H3 stays inside its parent H2 unless a justified split applies.

dimensions.name: Block count within 3–7 range
dimensions.description: Does the output contain between 3 and 7 blocks? Sessions with fewer than 3 blocks suggest under-splitting; more than 7 suggest over-splitting.

anchors.1: Output has fewer than 3 or more than 7 blocks. The session content clearly warranted more or fewer divisions but rules were not applied to enforce the range.
anchors.5: Block count is at the edge of the acceptable range (exactly 3 or exactly 7) where a better-reasoned division could have landed closer to the 4–6 ideal range.
anchors.10: Block count is between 3 and 7, and the specific number is the most natural fit for the session's content volume and topic count.

dimensions.name: Block size balance — 100 to 600 words per block
dimensions.description: Is each block within the 100–600 word target? Are blocks roughly comparable in depth — no single block dramatically larger or smaller than others?

anchors.1: At least one block has fewer than 100 words (stub block) or more than 600 words (overloaded block). Blocks are wildly unbalanced — one block covers 80% of the session content while others are near-empty.
anchors.5: Block sizes are mostly within range but one block is noticeably shorter (100–150 words on a simple intro) or longer (550–600 words, borderline). Balance is acceptable but not optimal.
anchors.10: All blocks fall comfortably within 100–600 words. Blocks are reasonably balanced in depth. No single block dominates the session. The learner experiences consistent effort per block.

dimensions.name: Block title quality — learner-facing and descriptive
dimensions.description: Are block titles clear, learner-facing descriptions of what the block contains? Do they avoid using raw heading text verbatim when that text is cryptic or numbered?

anchors.1: Block titles are raw heading text copied verbatim (e.g. '1. Prompt Engineering', 'Why Not One SDLC for All?') or are vague single words (e.g. 'Introduction', 'Overview'). A learner cannot tell what the block covers from the title alone.
anchors.5: Block titles are mostly descriptive but at least one is a direct copy of a cryptic heading or is slightly too short to be informative. Titles are not wrong but could be more learner-facing.
anchors.10: Every block title is a clear, learner-facing phrase that describes what the learner will understand after completing it (e.g. 'Why Software Development Models Exist', 'Understanding the Waterfall Model'). Cryptic numbered headings are rephrased.

dimensions.name: Image assignment correctness
dimensions.description: Is each image assigned to the block where its img tag appears in the source HTML? Are image src, alt, and caption fields populated correctly?

anchors.1: Images are missing from the output entirely, assigned to the wrong block, or the images array is empty for blocks that clearly contain images in the source HTML.
anchors.5: Most images are correctly assigned but at least one image is missing from its block, assigned to a neighbouring block, or has an empty src when the source clearly had one.
anchors.10: Every image from the source HTML is assigned to the exact block where it appears. src is correctly extracted. alt and caption fields are populated where source text provides them.

dimensions.name: Content completeness — no content lost
dimensions.description: Does every section of the source HTML appear in exactly one block? Is no content dropped, duplicated, or orphaned between blocks?

anchors.1: Visible sections of the source HTML are missing from all blocks — content was dropped. Or the same content appears in two blocks. The total content across all blocks does not add up to the source.
anchors.5: Nearly all content is present but a minor section (one small H3 or a short paragraph) was accidentally omitted or a small piece appears in two adjacent blocks.
anchors.10: Every section of the source HTML appears in exactly one block. No content is lost. No content is duplicated. The union of all block content_html fields equals the complete source HTML.
